claims.claim_text: The module presents a specific practice-cohort anecdote about a candidate creating all resources in the wrong cluster during a mock exam.
claims.span_hint: line 35 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: One engineer in a practice cohort created every resource in the wrong cluster during a mock exam because the terminal prompt looked familiar and the first task felt easy.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a mock exam, it is easy to create resources in the wrong cluster when the prompt looks familiar and the first task feels easy.
claims.rationale: This is a presented-as-real anecdote with no attributable source, so the teaching point should stay while the concrete unsourced story is removed.

claims.claim_text: CKA candidates are allowed to use the Kubernetes documentation site during the exam, including the built-in search on `kubernetes.io/docs` so long as they do not open external search results.
claims.span_hint: line 74 | section: The Exam Loop Is a Control System; line 185 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation; line 381 | section: Cumulative Readiness Review
claims.rationale: The Linux Foundation resources-allowed page explicitly permits Kubernetes documentation access and explicitly allows the on-site search while forbidding external search results.

claims.claim_text: A kubeconfig context groups cluster, namespace, and user information, and `kubectl` uses the current context by default.
claims.span_hint: line 78 | section: The Exam Loop Is a Control System; line 95 | section: Target Safety Before Speed
claims.rationale: The kubeconfig documentation directly states that each context has cluster, namespace, and user parameters and that kubectl uses the current context by default.

claims.claim_text: In kubeadm-based control plane environments, control plane static Pod manifests live under `/etc/kubernetes/manifests`, the kubelet watches that directory, and those static Pods run in `kube-system`.
claims.span_hint: line 119 | section: Target Safety Before Speed; line 317 | section: Worked Troubleshooting Example; line 375 | section: Cumulative Readiness Review; line 415 | section: Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The kubeadm implementation details page explicitly documents the manifest path, kubelet watch behavior, and `kube-system` namespace placement for kubeadm-managed control plane static Pods.

claims.claim_text: Static Pods are managed directly by kubelet, and kubelet attempts to create corresponding mirror Pods on the Kubernetes API server.
claims.span_hint: line 121 | section: Target Safety Before Speed; line 397 | section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.rationale: The static Pods task page explicitly says static Pods are managed by kubelet and that kubelet automatically tries to create a mirror Pod for each one.

claims.claim_text: The sentence comparing kind's default networking behavior against kubeadm's bootstrap visibility is too specific for the available allowlisted support.
claims.span_hint: line 129 | section: Target Safety Before Speed
claims.anchor_text: kind uses a lightweight default networking setup suitable for local development, while kubeadm exposes the control plane bootstrap path more directly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: kind and kubeadm expose different parts of a practice environment, so you should understand which environment you are using and which assumptions follow from it.
claims.rationale: The comparison is pedagogically reasonable, but the specific networking-versus-bootstrap phrasing is more precise than the allowlisted sources cleanly support.

claims.claim_text: Client dry-run can print the object that would be sent without creating it, which is why `kubectl create ... --dry-run=client -o yaml` works as a manifest skeleton workflow.
claims.span_hint: line 153 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation; line 377 | section: Cumulative Readiness Review; line 411 | section: Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The `kubectl create deployment` reference explicitly documents `--dry-run=client` as printing the object that would be sent without sending it.

claims.claim_text: YAML indentation is structural and YAML uses spaces rather than tabs for indentation-sensitive structure.
claims.span_hint: line 164 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation; line 379 | section: Cumulative Readiness Review
claims.rationale: This is a standards-level YAML claim, but the authoritative YAML specification is off the current trusted-domain allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl explain` retrieves field documentation from the API server's OpenAPI schema.
claims.span_hint: line 192 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation; line 395 | section: Did You Know, item 1
claims.rationale: The official `kubectl explain` reference says information about each field is retrieved from the server in OpenAPI format.

claims.claim_text: A Deployment's `.spec.selector` must match `.spec.template.metadata.labels`, so labels placed only on the top-level Deployment metadata do not define the managed Pod set.
claims.span_hint: line 213 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation; line 239 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation
claims.rationale: The Deployment documentation explicitly states that `.spec.selector` must match `.spec.template.metadata.labels`.

claims.claim_text: Service backends and endpoint publication depend on the Service selector matching Pods and on Pod readiness, so a Service can exist while still having no usable endpoints.
claims.span_hint: line 211 | section: Command Generation, YAML Editing, and Documentation; line 323 | section: Worked Troubleshooting Example; line 399 | section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice documentation says Services with selectors get EndpointSlices containing matching Pods and that Pod-backed endpoint readiness maps to the Pod `Ready` condition.

claims.claim_text: The module states as fact that a fast unverified task can silently earn no credit.
claims.span_hint: line 348 | section: Exam Strategy Under Time Pressure; line 401 | section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: The exam's time pressure makes verification more important, not less important, because a fast unverified task can silently earn no credit while also giving you false confidence.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The exam's time pressure makes verification more important, not less important, because a fast unverified task can leave you with false confidence about whether you met the requirement.
claims.rationale: The official exam docs do not publish this specific no-credit guarantee in this form, so the scoring-specific assertion should be reduced to the defensible operational lesson.

further_reading.title: Linux Foundation Resources Allowed for CKA and CKAD
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the authoritative exam-policy source for which documentation candidates may consult during the CKA.

further_reading.title: Organizing Cluster Access Using kubeconfig Files
further_reading.why_relevant: It directly supports the module's repeated context-safety guidance around clusters, users, namespaces, and current-context behavior.

further_reading.title: Create Static Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the primary upstream explanation of kubelet-managed static Pods and mirror Pod behavior, which the module uses in its control-plane troubleshooting examples.

notes: No shared citation pool exists yet for `k8s/cka/part0-environment`, so `section_pool_ref` is null and `source_ids` remain empty. I skipped quiz prompts, quiz answer prose, exercise steps, success-criteria checklists, diagrams, code blocks, and most connective exam-strategy heuristics unless they contained a distinct load-bearing factual claim that would need an inline citation or a rewrite disposition.
